title: 5.6 practice
description: Practice what you learned in lesson 5.6!

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which best describes the majority of goods transported along the Silk Road during the 13th and 14th centuries?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: luxury commodities traded among elites
innovative technologies shared across regions
staple crops essential for daily survival
medical equipment used by physicians

assessment_items.question_content:
**How was the rise of Islam connected to Silk Road trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Baghdad became a center of learning, and its ideas—including Islam—spread via Silk Road merchants.
The Mongols, hostile to Islam, blocked its spread along major trade routes.
Muslim caliphates profited from porcelain trade, boosting the spread of Islam.
Islamic tribes aggressively took control of Silk Road trade and forced merchants to convert.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What was one significant outcome of Western European trading fairs in the High Middle Ages?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: the emergence of early businesses and commercial partnerships
the outbreak of the Black Death through local trade networks
increased hostility toward Muslim merchants and travelers
the decline of long-distance trade along the Silk Road

assessment_items.question_content:
**Although expanding trade networks in the 14th century brought many benefits, what was one significant negative consequence?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Increased connectivity allowed diseases to spread quickly.
Trade routes brought a wave of cultural intolerance across all regions.
Trade led to the loss of all local traditions and customs.
Merchants became less important to society as trade increased.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did the collapse of the Han Dynasty in 220 CE affect trade along the Silk Road?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It slowed long-distance trade, but regional trade continued.
It had no effect because the Han rarely participated in Silk Road trade.
It completely stopped Silk Road trade, as no power could protect merchants.
It increased trade activity, since the Han had restricted commerce with taxes and bans.

assessment_items.question_content:
**In what ways did peaceful conditions in Rome and China contribute to the growth of trade networks?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They created stability that encouraged merchants to travel long distances safely.
They reduced the need for trade by encouraging self-sufficient economies.
They weakened central governments, allowing private traders to dominate commerce.
They isolated both empires from outside contact, limiting cultural and commercial exchange.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did silk production influence women’s roles in Han society?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: It gave women an important economic role through raising silkworms and weaving silk.
It allowed women to hold official government positions related to trade.
It caused women to become merchants who traveled along the Silk Road.
It led to women losing status because silk was considered men’s work.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why were pack animals such as camels essential to the success of long-distance trade routes?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: They could carry heavy loads across harsh environments where other animals struggled.
They were primarily raised as food supplies for traders during journeys.
They replaced the need for waterways and ships in all trade networks.
They were used mainly for ceremonial purposes to display wealth.

assessment_items.question_content:
**How did China’s conflict with the Xiongnu help open trade to the West?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: China sought horses to fight the Xiongnu, leading to new western trade routes.
Chinese soldiers taxed merchants in Xiongnu lands, boosting revenue.
China traveled west to buy cannons to defeat the Xiongnu.
The Xiongnu forced the Han to flee west, opening new trade paths.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What development in both Rome and China between 100 CE and 300 CE helped promote trade between the two empires?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both empires entered long periods of peace—Pax Romana and Pax Sinica.
Rome and China signed a formal treaty ensuring safe travel for merchants.
Both empires began domesticating camels for use in trade caravans.
Both empires adopted new religions that encouraged trade and exchange.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Why might the name "Silk Road" be considered misleading?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Silk was just one of many goods traded across the region.
The “roads” were actually a network of shifting, unmarked trade routes.
The trade routes were primarily maritime, not overland.
Silk was only traded within China and did not reach other regions.

assessment_items.question_content:
**What’s one way China’s current trade efforts are similar to how the Tang Dynasty boosted trade?**

[[☃ radio 1]]

choices.content: Both were focused on expanding trade routes to connect with distant markets.
Both relied mainly on sea power to control overseas colonies.
Both completely isolated their economies to protect domestic industries.
Both rejected foreign influence in order to protect cultural traditions.
